content.full_text:
¶

A.

RAVI MEHTA
CHAIRMAN

FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION

April 21, 1997

Cindy Thompson
Chief
Administrative and Information Services
Department of Consumer Affairs
400 R Street, Suite 1000
Sacramento, California 95814

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-97-204

Dear Ms. Thompson:

This letter is a response to your request for advice on behalf of Mr. Gates regarding the
"revolving door" provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

QUESTION

Do the "revolving door" provisions of the Act apply to a former legislative staff person,
Mr. Bill Gates, who served as his senator's alternate on the Seismic Safety Commission
("commission") and provided the commission with consulting and legal expertise?

CONCLUSION

The "revolving door" provisions of the Act do not apply to designated employees of the
Legislature. There is a question whether the "revolving door" provisions apply to Mr. Gates an
ex-officer, employee, or consultant of the commission since he served as the Senator's alternate
and provided legal and consulting services for the commission. However, since even an
affirmative answer to this question would not result in Mr. Gates being unable to work as a paid
consultant with the commission, we decline to address the issue.

FACTS

For the past 14 years, Mr. Bill Gates served as Senator Alfred E. Alquist's administrative

¹ Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109 -
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.

428 J STREET, SUITE 800, SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814 (916) 322-5660

File No. A-97-204
Page 2

assistant at the Senator's district office. Mr. Gates was a designated employee of the Legislature.
Within the past year, Senator Alquist was "termed out." During the final three years of his
tenure with Senator Alquist, Mr. Gates served on the commission as the Senator's alternate,
where he voted the Senator's views on the matters which came before the commission. Senator
Alquist assigned Mr. Gates many added responsibilities relating to issues before the commission.
In addition, Mr. Gates often gave the commission legal advice since he was an attorney and the
commission did not have its own legal counsel. Mr. Gates also helped the commission by
assisting in securing grants, contracts, and by helping to win an appeal which led to a money
judgment. Currently, the commission is trying to secure grants and is seeking a redirection of
federal money to the commission. The commission would like to procure the services of Mr.
Gates as a consultant to help in the above endeavor.

ANALYSIS

Section 87406 in pertinent part provides that:

"(d)(1) No designated employee of a state administrative agency,
any officer, employee, or consultant of a state administrative
agency who holds a position which entails the making, or
participation in the making, of decisions which may foreseeably
have a material effect on any financial interest, and no member of a
state administrative agency, for a period of one year after leaving
office or employment, shall, for compensation, act as agent or
attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person, by making
any formal or informal appearance, or by making any oral or
written communication, before any state administrative agency, or
officer or employee thereof, for which he or she worked or
represented during the 12 months before leaving office or
employment, if the appearance or communication is made for the
purpose of influencing administrative or legislative action, or
influencing any action or proceeding involving the issuance,
amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, grant,
contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property." (Emphasis
added.)

Section 87400 defines "state administrative agency" as every state office, department,
division, bureau, board and commission, but excludes the California Legislature. Thus, as a
former designated employee of the Legislature, Mr. Gates is not subject to the restrictions of
Section 87406. (Stenseth Advice Letter, No. A-93-320; Dudley Advice Letter, No. A-93-123.)

There is the issue of whether the "revolving door" provisions of the Act apply to Mr.
Gates, not as a designated employee of the legislature, but as an officer, employee or consultant
of the commission. Mr. Gates did serve as an alternate to Senator Alquist and voted, albeit
consistent with Senator Alquist's viewpoints. Also, Mr. Gates served as legal counsel to the

File No. A-97-204
Page 3

commission. In addition, Mr. Gates consulted with the Commission on different grant issues.

However, the Commission will not make a determination on this issue since Section 87406 would not prohibit Mr. Gates from contracting with the commission to be a consultant even if did apply to Mr. Gates in his capacity with the commission. Section 87406 would prohibit Mr. Gates for compensation, to act as agent or attorney for, or otherwise represent, any other person, but not the commission itself.

If you have any other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel

[signature]

By: Marte Castaños
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

SGC:MC:ak

CONCLUSION: The “revolving door” provisions of the Act do not apply to designated employees of the
